subUnits.title: 10.1 Major Spheres & Interior Structure of the Earth
notes: Four Major Spheres of Earth:
1. Lithosphere (Rock Sphere):
• Solid outer layer: crust and upper mantle
• Includes continents, ocean floors, soils, rocks
• Thickness: 5-70 km (oceanic thin, continental thick)
2. Hydrosphere (Water Sphere):
• All water on Earth: oceans, seas, rivers, lakes, glaciers, groundwater
• Covers ~71% of Earth's surface
• Regulates climate and supports life
3. Atmosphere (Air Sphere):
• Layer of gases surrounding Earth
• Composition: 78% Nitrogen, 21% Oxygen, 1% others (Argon, CO₂, etc.)
• Divided into layers: Troposphere, Stratosphere, Mesosphere, Thermosphere, Exosphere
• Protects from UV radiation, regulates temperature
4. Biosphere (Life Sphere):
• Zone where life exists: overlaps lithosphere, hydrosphere, atmosphere
• Includes all plants, animals, microorganisms, and ecosystems
• Dependent on interactions between other three spheres
Interior Structure of the Earth:
1. Crust:
• Outermost layer, 5-70 km thick
• Continental crust: granite-rich (SIAL: Silicon + Aluminium), lighter, thicker
• Oceanic crust: basalt-rich (SIMA: Silicon + Magnesium), denser, thinner
• Divided into tectonic plates
2. Mantle:
• Lies below crust, ~2,900 km thick
• Upper mantle: includes asthenosphere (semi-molten, plastic)
• Lower mantle: solid but flows slowly over geological time
• Composition: silicate minerals rich in iron and magnesium
• Convection currents drive plate tectonics
3. Core:
• Innermost layer, radius ~3,480 km
• Outer core: liquid iron and nickel, generates Earth's magnetic field
• Inner core: solid iron and nickel due to extreme pressure
• Temperature: ~4,000-5,500°C (similar to Sun's surface)
• Pressure: millions of times atmospheric pressure at surface
Relationship Between Spheres:
• Constant interaction: weathering (atmosphere + hydrosphere → lithosphere), photosynthesis (atmosphere + hydrosphere → biosphere), volcanic activity (lithosphere → atmosphere)
• Human activities impact all spheres (pollution, deforestation, climate change)

quiz.q: The solid outer layer of Earth containing continents and ocean floors is the:
quiz.explanation: Lithosphere is the rocky outer shell comprising crust and upper mantle, including landforms and ocean basins.

quiz.q: Earth's atmosphere is composed mainly of:
quiz.explanation: Atmosphere is ~78% nitrogen, ~21% oxygen, ~1% other gases including argon, CO₂, and trace gases.

quiz.q: The layer of Earth responsible for generating the magnetic field is the:
quiz.explanation: Liquid iron and nickel in the outer core create convection currents that generate Earth's magnetic field.

quiz.q: Continental crust is mainly composed of:
quiz.explanation: Continental crust is granitic, rich in silicon and aluminium (SIAL), making it lighter and thicker than oceanic crust.

quiz.q: The asthenosphere is located in the:
quiz.explanation: Asthenosphere is the semi-molten, plastic part of the upper mantle that allows tectonic plates to move.

quiz.q: The biosphere includes:
opts: All living organisms and their environments
quiz.explanation: Biosphere encompasses all life forms and their interactions with lithosphere, hydrosphere, and atmosphere.

quiz.q: Oceanic crust is thinner and denser than continental crust because it is rich in:
quiz.explanation: Oceanic crust is basaltic (SIMA), denser and thinner (~5-10 km) compared to granitic continental crust (~30-70 km).

quiz.q: Convection currents in the mantle cause:
quiz.explanation: Heat-driven convection in the asthenosphere moves tectonic plates, causing earthquakes, volcanoes, and mountain building.

quiz.q: The inner core is solid despite high temperatures because of:
quiz.explanation: Immense pressure at Earth's center keeps inner core iron-nickel solid despite temperatures ~5,000°C.

quiz.q: Which sphere regulates Earth's temperature and protects from UV radiation?
quiz.explanation: Atmosphere contains ozone layer (blocks UV) and greenhouse gases (regulate heat), maintaining habitable temperatures.

subUnits.title: 10.2 Rock Types: Igneous, Sedimentary & Metamorphic
notes: Rocks are naturally occurring aggregates of minerals. Classified by formation process:
1. Igneous Rocks:
• Formed from cooling and solidification of magma/lava
• Intrusive (Plutonic): Cool slowly underground → large crystals (e.g., Granite, Gabbro)
• Extrusive (Volcanic): Cool quickly on surface → fine crystals/glassy (e.g., Basalt, Obsidian, Pumice)
• Characteristics: crystalline, no fossils, interlocking grains
2. Sedimentary Rocks:
• Formed from accumulation, compaction, and cementation of sediments
• Types:
- Mechanically formed: from weathered rock fragments (e.g., Sandstone, Conglomerate)
- Chemically formed: from mineral precipitation (e.g., Limestone, Rock salt, Gypsum)
- Organically formed: from plant/animal remains (e.g., Coal, Chalk)
• Characteristics: layered (stratified), may contain fossils, often porous/permeable
3. Metamorphic Rocks:
• Formed when existing rocks change under heat, pressure, or chemically active fluids
• Examples:
- Limestone → Marble
- Shale → Slate
- Granite → Gneiss
- Sandstone → Quartzite
- Coal → Anthracite
• Characteristics: foliated/banded, recrystallized, harder than parent rock, rarely contain fossils
Rock Properties:
• Structure: crystalline, layered, foliated, glassy
• Colour: depends on mineral composition (e.g., quartz=clear, feldspar=pink, mica=black)
• Texture: grain size (fine, medium, coarse), arrangement
• Permeability: ability to transmit water (sandstone=high, granite=low, limestone=variable)
The Rock Cycle:
• Continuous process where rocks transform from one type to another
• Igneous → weathering → sediment → sedimentary → heat/pressure → metamorphic → melting → magma → igneous
• Driven by plate tectonics, erosion, and Earth's internal heat

quiz.q: Rocks formed from cooling magma or lava are called:
quiz.explanation: Igneous rocks crystallize from molten rock (magma underground, lava on surface).

quiz.q: Which rock is an example of intrusive igneous rock?
quiz.explanation: Granite cools slowly underground, forming large visible crystals, classifying it as intrusive igneous.

quiz.q: Sedimentary rocks are characterized by:
quiz.explanation: Sedimentary rocks form in layers (strata) from compressed sediments and often preserve fossils.

quiz.q: Limestone transforms into marble through:
quiz.explanation: Heat and pressure recrystallize limestone into marble, a classic metamorphic transformation.

quiz.q: Which rock type is most likely to be permeable?
quiz.explanation: Sandstone has porous spaces between cemented sand grains, allowing water to pass through easily.

quiz.q: The rock cycle describes:
quiz.explanation: Rock cycle shows how igneous, sedimentary, and metamorphic rocks interconvert through geological processes.

quiz.q: Foliation is a characteristic of:
quiz.explanation: Foliation (banded/layered appearance) forms in metamorphic rocks due to directed pressure during metamorphism.

quiz.q: Which process forms chemically precipitated sedimentary rocks?
quiz.explanation: Chemical sedimentary rocks like rock salt and gypsum form when dissolved minerals precipitate as water evaporates.

quiz.q: Obsidian has a glassy texture because it:
quiz.explanation: Rapid cooling of lava prevents crystal growth, resulting in a smooth, glassy volcanic rock.

quiz.q: Which statement about permeability is TRUE?
opts: Sandstone allows water to pass through
Marble is more permeable than sandstone
quiz.explanation: Sandstone's grain structure creates pore spaces, making it permeable; granite and marble are crystalline and impermeable.
